food_subgroup: Cooked mutton / goat meat dish — dry-fried spiced preparation

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Aattukkari Milagu Varuval. FOODON_03302051 (goat meat food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers goat meat products broadly; no specific code exists for this dry-fried pepper preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Aattukkari Milagu Varuval or equivalent South Indian mutton pepper fry preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Aattukkari Milagu Varuval is approximately 250 g, comprising boneless mutton pieces coated in black pepper and spice masala, served as a main dish or side with rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton (goat), cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN values for cooked goat/mutton meat used as the primary base. Adjustments applied for oil absorption (~5 g oil per 100 g cooked dish) and spice contribution (black pepper, curry leaves, chilli) consistent with standard South Indian dry-fry preparation. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. Sodium estimated from typical recipe salt addition (~400 mg/100 g). This is an adapted estimate; no exact ICMR-NIN entry for Aattukkari Milagu Varuval exists.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of meat-based dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked goat/mutton meat (GI ~0–15, negligible carbohydrate); University of Sydney GI Database reference for meat foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Aattukkari Milagu Varuval. Meat-based dishes with negligible carbohydrate content have a GI of approximately 0–15 per University of Sydney GI Database conventions for protein/fat-dominant foods. GI set to 10 as a conservative low estimate. GL per 250 g serving = (10 × 3.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 0.8. gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg/100 g) from salt and spice marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced salt preparation, as a 250 g serving contributes approximately 1050 mg sodium, representing nearly half the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.2 g/100 g) and low glycemic index (~10)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Aattukkari Milagu Varuval is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions, as its negligible carbohydrate load and low GI produce minimal postprandial glycemic excursion; however, total fat and caloric density should be considered in overall meal planning.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.9 g/100 g) and cholesterol (~72 mg/100 g) from mutton, combined with moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in limited portions (≤150 g) and infrequently, as the saturated fat and cholesterol content of mutton can contribute to LDL elevation; choosing leaner cuts and minimising added oil during preparation is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aattukkari Milagu Varuval is a classic South Indian dry-fried mutton dish originating from Tamil Nadu cuisine. The name translates literally as 'goat meat pepper fry' in Tamil — 'aattukkari' meaning goat/mutton, 'milagu' meaning black pepper, and 'varuval' meaning fry or roast. The dish is prepared by marinating boneless goat meat pieces in a blend of black pepper, turmeric, red chilli, ginger, garlic, fennel seeds, and coriander, then sautéing them in oil with fresh curry leaves until the meat is cooked through and coated in a fragrant, slightly crisp spice crust. The result is a bold, intensely spiced, dry preparation with a dominant peppery heat, aromatic curry leaf fragrance, and tender yet slightly chewy meat texture. It is served as a main course or side dish alongside rice, parotta, or roti in South Indian and Tamil restaurant contexts, including in Singapore's Indian restaurant scene. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Per 100 g it provides approximately 218 kcal, 18.5 g protein, 14.8 g fat, and only 3.2 g carbohydrate, making it glycemically benign (GI ~10). The high protein content supports satiety and muscle maintenance. Sodium is moderately elevated (~420 mg/100 g) due to salt in the marinade. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is typically halal when prepared with goat meat. Clinically, it is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low carbohydrate and low GI profile, but saturated fat and sodium content warrant moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease or hypertension.
common_user_queries: Is mutton pepper fry good for diabetes?
South Indian mutton pepper fry recipe

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for Aattukkari Milagu Varuval as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked goat/mutton meat data with standard adjustments for oil absorption and spice contribution typical of South Indian dry-fry preparations. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database conventions for meat-dominant low-carbohydrate dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base ingredient (cooked goat meat) is well-characterised in ICMR-NIN and the preparation method is standard.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on ICMR-NIN cooked goat/mutton meat data; no exact published entry for Aattukkari Milagu Varuval exists in approved databases. 2. Sodium (~420 mg/100 g) is estimated from typical recipe salt addition for South Indian dry-fry dishes; actual restaurant values may vary. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for cooked goat meat. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for cooked goat meat. 5. GI is set to 10 as an analogous estimate for a meat-dominant, low-carbohydrate dish per University of Sydney GI Database conventions; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists. 6. The dish is served at Manam - Upper Circular Road on field survey data at SGD 29.00. 7. Halal status is assumed based on typical South Indian Muslim restaurant preparation with goat meat; verification with the specific restaurant is recommended. 8. trans_fat_g (0.2 g/100 g) reflects naturally occurring trans fats in ruminant meat (conjugated linoleic acid and vaccenic acid) as reported in ICMR-NIN and USDA data for goat/mutton.
